doctrines.rule: Agreement to a conspiracy need not be express and may be inferred from a coordinated course of conduct that has no plausible innocent explanation.
levers: frequency of communication spiked around criminal events
financial payments disguised as legitimate business expenses
coordinated movements tracked by cell tower data
incriminating statements made in covert recordings
traps: Students conclude no conspiracy exists because there is no 'smoking gun' conversation or written contract.
Students assume individual acts must be criminal in themselves to support an inference of agreement.
cases: The Dan Markel Murder Case Study

doctrines.rule: In most jurisdictions, a conspiracy conviction requires that at least one conspirator commit an act in furtherance of the agreement, though the act itself may be legal and trivial.
doctrines.split: MPC § 5.03(5) waives the overt act requirement for first and second-degree felonies; 18 U.S.C. § 371 requires it for all federal conspiracies.
levers: whether the act was a legal activity like checking out a library book
whether the act was committed by the defendant or a co-conspirator
the severity of the target felony
traps: Students apply the 'substantial step' test from attempt law instead of the 'any act' standard for conspiracy.
Students conclude an overt act is insufficient because the act itself was not illegal.

doctrines.rule: The bilateral approach requires two or more genuine criminal minds, while the unilateral approach permits conviction if the defendant believed they were agreeing with another, even if that person was a feigning government agent.
doctrines.split: Traditional jurisdictions (State v. Pacheco) require bilateral agreement; MPC jurisdictions and a majority of reformed codes (Garcia v. State) allow unilateral agreement.
levers: whether the only other party to the agreement was an undercover officer
whether the jurisdiction follows the MPC or common law
the defendant's subjective belief regarding the agent's intent
traps: Students conclude a conspiracy exists in a bilateral jurisdiction when the only co-conspirator is an informant.
Students confuse the unilateral rule with the defense of entrapment.

doctrines.rule: To be convicted of conspiracy, a defendant must specifically intend that every element of the target offense be accomplished, even if the target offense itself requires only a lesser mental state.
doctrines.split: The Pond majority requires specific intent for all elements (including aggravating factors); the Pond dissent argues the mens rea for conspiracy should match the mens rea of the completed offense.
levers: whether the defendant knew of an aggravating factor like a weapon
whether the defendant intended for a specific result (e.g., death) to occur
whether the target crime is a strict liability or general intent offense
traps: Students assume that because a principal is guilty of a crime, the conspirator is automatically guilty of conspiracy to commit that specific degree of crime.
Students forget that conspiracy is a specific intent crime regardless of the target crime's mens rea.

doctrines.rule: A defendant cannot be convicted of conspiracy to commit a crime of negligence because one cannot specifically intend to accomplish an unintended result.
levers: whether the target crime is involuntary manslaughter
whether the parties agreed to engage in risky conduct without intending the harmful result
traps: Students conclude a conspiracy exists because the parties agreed to the underlying dangerous conduct, missing the requirement of intent for the result.

doctrines.rule: A supplier of legal goods or services who knows they are being used for a crime is only a conspirator if they act with the purpose of promoting the criminal enterprise.
levers: whether the supplier charged inflated prices
whether the supplier had a 'stake in the venture' (e.g., commissions)
whether the goods have no legitimate use
traps: Students equate knowledge of criminal use with the intent to join a conspiracy.
Students fail to distinguish between a misdemeanor (requires purpose) and an aggravated felony (where knowledge may suffice).

doctrines.rule: In cases involving serious or 'aggravated' crimes, a conspirator's intent to further the enterprise may be inferred from knowledge alone, even without a financial stake.
doctrines.split: Lauria suggests this inference for 'grave' crimes but notes it does not apply to misdemeanors like prostitution.
levers: the severity of the crime (e.g., arson vs. prostitution)
the likelihood that the goods provided would be used for the specific grave crime
traps: Students apply the knowledge-only inference to minor offenses.
Students assume a financial stake is always required for a supplier to be a conspirator.

doctrines.rule: A conspirator is vicariously liable for the substantive crimes committed by co-conspirators if those crimes were foreseeable and committed in furtherance of the conspiracy.
doctrines.split: The Pinkerton doctrine is followed in federal court and some states; the MPC and a majority of reformed states reject it in favor of traditional accomplice liability.
levers: whether the crime was a 'natural consequence' of the agreement
whether the defendant was incarcerated at the time of the substantive crime
whether the jurisdiction follows the MPC
traps: Students apply Pinkerton liability in an MPC jurisdiction.
Students conclude a defendant is liable for a co-conspirator's 'frolic' that was not in furtherance of the shared goal.

doctrines.rule: A single conspiracy (a 'rimmed wheel') exists only if the various 'spokes' (participants) are aware of each other and have a community of interest in the overall venture.
levers: whether the 'spokes' knew of each other's existence
whether the success of one spoke depended on the success of the others
whether there was a common overall plan
traps: Students conclude a single conspiracy exists just because there is a common central figure (the 'hub').
Students miss the prejudicial effect of 'guilt transference' when multiple conspiracies are tried as one.

doctrines.rule: Agreement among competitors may be inferred when they all adhere to a plan knowing that concerted action is invited and that cooperation is essential to the plan's success.
levers: whether all parties received the same proposal naming the other addressees
whether the proposed action was a radical departure from prior business practice
whether the action would be unprofitable without unanimous participation
traps: Students conclude no conspiracy exists because the parties only communicated with a central figure, not each other.

doctrines.rule: A simple agreement to buy or sell contraband does not constitute a conspiracy unless there is evidence of a shared purpose to further the distribution of drugs beyond the immediate sale.
levers: the presence of 'fronting' (credit arrangements)
a prolonged course of dealing
the use of coded language
traps: Students conclude every drug sale is a conspiracy.
Students miss that 'fronting' is strong evidence of a shared stake in the venture.

doctrines.rule: An agreement to commit a crime that by definition requires two participants (e.g., dueling, bigamy) cannot be prosecuted as a conspiracy.
doctrines.split: The Supreme Court treats this as a judicial presumption that legislatures can override (Iannelli v. United States).
levers: whether more than the logically necessary number of people participated
whether the target crime is bribery, adultery, or bigamy
traps: Students apply Wharton's Rule to crimes that *can* be committed by one person but happened to be committed by two.
Students forget the 'third-party exception' (if a third person joins, the rule doesn't apply).

doctrines.rule: A person belonging to a class of people the legislature intended to protect or exempt from a substantive crime cannot be prosecuted for conspiring to commit that crime.
levers: whether the statute targets only one party to a bilateral transaction (e.g., the transporter in the Mann Act)
whether the defendant is the 'victim' the statute seeks to protect
traps: Students conclude a woman is a co-conspirator for 'agreeing' to be transported in violation of the Mann Act.

doctrines.rule: Under traditional law, conspiracy does not merge with the completed offense; under the MPC, a defendant cannot be convicted of both conspiracy and the target crime.
doctrines.split: Traditional jurisdictions allow cumulative punishment; MPC Section 1.07(1)(b) requires merger unless the conspiracy has objectives beyond the completed crime.
levers: whether the jurisdiction follows the MPC
whether the defendant successfully completed the target offense
traps: Students assume a defendant cannot be convicted of both conspiracy and the completed crime because of double jeopardy, missing the traditional non-merger rule.

doctrines.rule: Withdrawal from a conspiracy requires an affirmative act to defeat the conspiracy's purpose or communication of the withdrawal to all co-conspirators.
doctrines.split: Common law withdrawal only terminates liability for future crimes; MPC withdrawal can be a complete defense to the conspiracy charge if the defendant thwarts the success of the conspiracy.
levers: whether the defendant notified the police
whether the defendant communicated their departure to all co-conspirators
whether the defendant successfully prevented the crime
traps: Students conclude a defendant has withdrawn simply by ceasing to participate without taking affirmative steps to thwart the conspiracy.

doctrines.rule: In drug conspiracies, a participant is liable for the total quantity of drugs handled by the conspiracy that was reasonably foreseeable to them.
levers: the defendant's role (organizer vs. peripheral participant)
the defendant's knowledge of the scale of the operation
whether the quantity was a 'natural consequence' of the agreement
traps: Students assume a minor participant (the 'girlfriend problem') is only sentenced for the drugs they personally handled, forgetting the scope of vicarious attribution.

cases.name: State v. Pond
cases.holding: A defendant must specifically intend that every element of the planned offense be accomplished, including aggravating factors like the use of a weapon.
cases.facts_summary: Defendant acted as a lookout for a robbery where his accomplices gestured as if they had a gun, but there was no evidence the defendant agreed to or intended the use of a weapon.

cases.name: People v. Lauria
cases.holding: Knowledge of a customer's criminal activity is insufficient for conspiracy liability unless the supplier has a stake in the venture or the crime is of an aggravated nature.
cases.facts_summary: The owner of a telephone answering service knew some of his clients were prostitutes using the service for business but charged them standard rates.

cases.name: Pinkerton v. United States
cases.holding: A conspirator is liable for substantive offenses committed by a co-conspirator in furtherance of the conspiracy, even if they did not participate in or know of the specific acts.
cases.facts_summary: One brother committed tax offenses while the other was in prison; the imprisoned brother was held liable for the substantive crimes based on their ongoing conspiracy.

cases.name: Interstate Circuit v. United States
cases.holding: Agreement can be inferred when multiple parties adhere to a plan knowing that concerted action is required for success and that others have been invited to join.
cases.facts_summary: Eight film distributors simultaneously adopted restrictive policies after receiving a letter from a theater chain that addressed all of them together.

cases.name: Kotteakos v. United States
cases.holding: A single conspiracy cannot be proven by showing multiple separate agreements with a common hub if the 'spokes' have no knowledge of or connection to each other.
cases.facts_summary: Thirty-two defendants were charged in one conspiracy for obtaining fraudulent loans through the same broker, though the borrowers were independent of one another.

blocks.summary: Introduction to conspiracy and the Dan Markel case study, illustrating inferring agreement and vicarious liability.
MPC and Federal statutes on conspiracy, breaking down the basic elements including the overt act requirement.
Analysis of the split between bilateral and unilateral approaches to conspiracy involving undercover agents.
State v. Pond: Majority and dissent on whether conspiracy requires specific intent for all elements of the target offense.
Note on the impossibility of conspiring to commit crimes of negligence.
Discussion questions applying the Pond holding to the Markel case facts.
People v. Lauria: The seminal case on the supplier problem and the knowledge vs. purpose distinction.
Discussion questions and table applying Lauria to Purdue Pharma and social media platforms.
Pinkerton v. United States: Majority and dissent on vicarious liability for co-conspirators' crimes.
Bank robbery hypothetical and the MPC's rejection of Pinkerton liability.
Interstate Circuit v. United States: Inferring agreement from parallel conduct and mutual awareness.
Kotteakos v. United States: Defining the limits of a single conspiracy in 'wheel and spoke' patterns.
Note on United States v. Alvarez and the sufficiency of a 'nod and a smile' for agreement.
Structural limits on conspiracy: Buyer-Seller exception, Wharton's Rule, and the Gebardi principle.
Corporate conspiracy and the Purdue Pharma case study on individual vs. entity accountability.
Drug stings, manufactured conspiracy, and the 'girlfriend problem' in sentencing.
Summary table comparing Traditional and MPC approaches to merger and withdrawal.
Critical analysis of how conspiracy rules interact with civil asset forfeiture and proactive policing.

hypo_seeds.description: A defendant agrees to 'scare' someone but claims they never intended for the person to be killed.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests whether specific intent for the result (death) is required for conspiracy to commit murder.

hypo_seeds.description: A social media platform algorithmically amplifies criminal content that drives engagement and ad revenue.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests the 'stake in the venture' and 'aggravated crime' inferences from Lauria.

hypo_seeds.description: A ringleader hires two different people to rob two different banks; the robbers know of each other but never meet.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests the scope of vicarious liability for co-conspirators' crimes.

hypo_seeds.description: A day laborer nods and smiles when asked if he will be at a drug unloading site.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests the absolute minimum threshold for inferring agreement from conduct.

hypo_seeds.description: A woman voluntarily travels with a man across state lines in a jurisdiction where only the transporter is criminalized.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests the Gebardi principle regarding protected classes and conspiracy.

hypo_seeds.description: A girlfriend of a drug dealer is charged with conspiracy based on the total quantity of drugs sold by the entire ring.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests the 'girlfriend problem' and the attribution of drug quantities for sentencing.

hypo_seeds.description: Corporate executives avoid felony charges by pleading to strict liability misbranding while the entity pleads to conspiracy.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests the application of conspiracy to corporate actors vs. individuals.
